title: Gimmes: the putt that was never missed
microFact: A 'gimme' is a short putt your partners concede. Legal in match play, never in stroke play — and knowing the difference matters.
lesson:
"That's good" — three words that mean your short putt is CONCEDED: pick it up, count one stroke, no need to putt it.

The rules reality:
• MATCH PLAY: concessions are fully legal and strategic. Once conceded, it can't be withdrawn or refused.
• STROKE PLAY: NO gimmes exist. Every ball must be holed. Picking up a "gimme" in a medal round = penalty territory.
• CASUAL golf: gimmes "inside the leather" (closer than a putter grip's length) keep things moving and friendly.

Gimme etiquette for the newcomer:
1. Never ASK for a gimme. Ever. You wait to be given.
2. When given one, a small "thanks" and pick up briskly.
3. Give them generously to others on genuinely short putts — stingy gimme-givers are noted.
4. In anything with a trophy or money: hole everything, and quietly expect the same.

One warning: don't let casual gimmes rot your short putting. Practice 1-meter putts anyway — competition day exposes everyone whose 'automatic' putts were never actually hit.

quiz.question: In which format are conceded putts (gimmes) actually legal under the Rules?
quiz.explanation: Concession is a formal part of match play only. In stroke play and Stableford you must hole out (or pick up for zero points in Stableford).
